lessons.title: Why Signals Get Weaker With Distance
lessons.content:
Have you noticed your phone shows full bars in one spot and barely any a block away? **Radio propagation** is the story of what happens to a radio signal on its journey from the **transmitter** (the thing that sends the signal) to the **receiver** (the thing that listens for it), traveling through the real world of open air, cities, and weather.

The first and biggest part of that story is simple: a signal gets **weaker the farther it travels**. Here is the picture. Imagine a single light bulb in a dark field. Right next to it, it is blinding. Walk away and it dims; far off it is just a faint dot. The bulb is not making less light. The light is just **spread out** over a bigger and bigger area as it moves away, so any one spot, like your eye, catches a smaller and smaller share.

A radio transmitter does the exact same thing. It pours out a fixed amount of power, and that power spreads outward in an ever-growing bubble. The receiver is like a small bucket trying to catch rain: close to the source the rain is heavy and the bucket fills fast; far away the same rain is spread thin over a huge area and the bucket barely catches a drop. This spreading is the reason a signal fades with distance, even with nothing in the way. It is called **spreading loss** (or free-space loss, meaning the loss you get in clear, empty space with no obstacles).

The spreading is steep: it follows what is called the **inverse-square idea**. In plain words, when you go **twice as far**, the same power is spread over about **four times** the area, so the signal is only about **a quarter** as strong. Go ten times as far and it is roughly a hundredth as strong. The signal never quite reaches zero, it just keeps getting fainter, which is why a powerful enough transmitter can still be heard very far away.

**Recap:** A radio signal weakens with distance because a fixed amount of power spreads over a growing area, like a bulb dimming as you walk away. Twice as far means roughly a quarter the strength. This spreading happens even in clear empty space, before the environment adds any extra trouble.

items.prompt: What does 'radio propagation' mean?
choices: The story of what happens to a radio signal as it travels from the transmitter to the receiver through the real world
The circuit that generates the radio signal in the first place
The process of choosing which radio station to listen to
The way a receiver turns a radio wave back into sound
items.explanation: Propagation is about the journey: how a signal spreads, weakens, bounces, and bends on its way from the sender (transmitter) to the listener (receiver). Generating the signal and decoding it at the end are separate jobs.

items.prompt: Why does a radio signal get weaker the farther it travels, even in clear empty space?
choices: The transmitter automatically lowers its power over distance
A fixed amount of power spreads out over a bigger and bigger area, so any one spot catches a smaller share
The radio wave slows down and runs out of energy
Air is heavy and crushes the wave flat
items.explanation: This is spreading loss. The transmitter keeps pouring out the same power, but that power fans out over an ever-growing bubble, so a small receiver intercepts less and less of it. The transmitter is not dialing down, and the wave does not 'run out' of energy.

items.prompt: A light bulb is used as an everyday picture of spreading loss. What is the key parallel?
choices: The bulb makes less light the longer it is on, like a transmitter wearing out
The bulb's light spreads over a larger area as you walk away, so it looks dimmer even though it makes the same light
The bulb changes color with distance, like a signal changing frequency
The bulb only shines in one direction, like a narrow radio beam
items.explanation: The bulb keeps making the same amount of light; it just looks dimmer far away because the light is spread thinner over a bigger area. A radio signal weakens with distance for exactly this reason.

items.prompt: Roughly how much weaker is a free-space signal when you move twice as far from the transmitter?
choices: About half as strong
About a quarter as strong
Exactly the same strength
It drops all the way to zero
items.explanation: This is the inverse-square idea in words: twice the distance spreads the same power over about four times the area, so the signal is roughly one quarter as strong. It does not simply halve, and it never quite reaches zero.

items.prompt: The term 'free-space loss' refers to the weakening of a signal:
choices: Caused only by hills and buildings blocking the path
Caused purely by the signal spreading out in clear, empty space with nothing in the way
That happens when a receiver is switched off
That only occurs underwater
items.explanation: Free-space loss is the baseline weakening from spreading alone, in clear empty space. Obstacles like hills and buildings add extra loss on top of this, but free-space loss is there even with a perfectly clear path.

items.prompt: Comparing the receiver to a bucket catching rain, why does a far-away receiver pick up so little signal?
choices: The rain (signal) stops falling past a certain distance
Far away the same rain is spread thin over a huge area, so a small bucket catches very little
The bucket shrinks as it moves away
Rain only falls near its source and never spreads
items.explanation: Up close the 'rain' of signal power is concentrated, so a fixed-size bucket fills quickly. Far away that same power is spread thin over a large area, so the same bucket catches only a trickle. The bucket size does not change; the spreading does.

items.prompt: Why can a very powerful transmitter still be heard at huge distances even though signals weaken with distance?
choices: Because powerful signals do not spread out at all
Because the signal fades but never quite reaches zero, so a strong enough start can still leave a usable trickle far away
Because power makes the wave travel faster and skip the weakening
Because distance does not affect strong signals
items.explanation: Spreading makes the signal fainter and fainter but never exactly zero. Starting with more power means there is still enough left to detect at a great distance. Distance weakens every signal, strong or weak; a strong one simply has more to spare.

items.prompt: Which everyday observation is best explained by spreading loss?
choices: A bonfire feels hot up close and cooler as you step back, even though it burns the same
A glass of water freezes when put in a freezer
A magnet sticks to a fridge door
A ball rolls downhill
items.explanation: The fire pours out the same heat, but that heat spreads over a larger area the farther you stand, so you feel less of it. This is the same spreading-with-distance idea that weakens a radio signal. The other options involve unrelated effects.

items.prompt: If you move ten times farther from a transmitter in free space, the signal is roughly:
choices: Ten times weaker
About a hundred times weaker
Unchanged
Twice as strong
items.explanation: With the inverse-square idea, ten times the distance spreads the power over about a hundred times the area, so the signal is roughly one hundredth as strong. The weakening grows much faster than the distance itself.

lessons.title: Blocking, Shadows, and Bending Around Edges
lessons.content:
Spreading loss is the clean, ideal story. The real world adds trouble, and the first kind is **blocking**. A hill, a building, a thick stand of trees, or even a tunnel wall can stand between the transmitter and the receiver and soak up or stop much of the signal. This is why your phone loses signal in a tunnel or a basement, and why a distant radio tower fades when you drive behind a ridge. The blocked region behind an obstacle is called the **shadow**, just like the dark patch behind something blocking the sun.

But here is the surprising part: a radio shadow is usually not completely dark. Try shouting to a friend who is just around the corner of a building. You cannot see them, yet they can still hear you, a little muffled. Sound **bends around the edge** of the corner to reach them. Radio waves do the same thing. When a wave hits the edge of an obstacle, part of it **bends around the edge** and leaks into the shadow behind. This bending around edges and corners is called **diffraction**.

Diffraction is why you often still get a weak signal behind a hill or building, not nothing. The signal that bends into the shadow is weaker than a clear path would give, but it is there. The sharper and higher the obstacle, and the deeper you are in the shadow, the weaker that bent-around signal becomes.

So a real signal faces two things at once: the steady weakening from spreading, plus extra loss whenever something blocks the path, softened a bit by diffraction sneaking energy into the shadows. A clear, unobstructed path (often called **line of sight**, meaning you could draw a straight clear line from transmitter to receiver) is always best. The more clutter in the way, the more the signal is shadowed and the faster it fades.

**Recap:** Obstacles like hills, buildings, and tunnels block signals and cast radio shadows, which is why you lose service in a tunnel. Diffraction lets waves bend around edges and over hilltops into the shadow, so the shadow is usually weak rather than truly dead. A clear line-of-sight path is best.

items.prompt: Why do you usually lose phone signal inside a tunnel?
choices: Tunnels are too far from every transmitter on Earth
The surrounding rock and walls block and absorb the radio signal, leaving you in a radio shadow
Radio waves cannot exist underground at all
Your phone turns off its antenna in tunnels
items.explanation: Thick rock and concrete block the signal, casting a radio shadow where little gets through. It is blocking by the environment, not distance or a magic underground rule. The phone keeps trying; the signal just cannot reach it well.

items.prompt: What is diffraction, in plain terms?
choices: A wave speeding up when it hits an obstacle
A wave bending around the edges of obstacles, so some energy reaches the shadow behind them
A wave splitting into different colors
A wave stopping completely at any barrier
items.explanation: Diffraction is the bending of waves around edges and corners. It is why some signal leaks into the shadow behind a hill or building. The wave does not speed up, split into colors, or fully stop at the edge.

items.prompt: You can hear a friend shouting from around the corner of a building even though you cannot see them. How does this relate to radio?
choices: It does not; sound and radio behave in totally opposite ways
Both sound and radio waves bend around the edge (diffract), so some reaches the shadowed spot you are standing in
The sound travels through your phone's speaker
It proves walls make signals stronger
items.explanation: Sound bending around the corner to reach you is everyday diffraction. Radio waves bend around edges in the same way, which is why a hidden receiver behind an obstacle often still picks up a weak signal.

items.prompt: A receiver sits just behind a tall ridge, out of direct sight of the tower. Thanks to diffraction, what does it most likely get?
choices: A perfectly strong signal, as if the ridge were not there
A weak but usable signal that bent over the ridge into the shadow
Absolutely nothing, since the ridge fully blocks all radio
A signal stronger than line of sight
items.explanation: Diffraction bends some energy over the ridge into the shadow, so the receiver typically gets a weak signal rather than nothing. It is weaker than a clear path would give, never stronger.

items.prompt: What does 'line of sight' mean for a radio link?
choices: The transmitter and receiver are the same color
You could draw a straight, clear, unobstructed line from the transmitter to the receiver
The signal travels only at night
The receiver is pointed at the sun
items.explanation: Line of sight means a clear straight path with nothing blocking it, the ideal situation for a strong signal. Obstacles in that path create shadows and extra loss.

items.prompt: A 'radio shadow' is:
choices: The dark area you see behind a radio tower at sunset
The weakened region behind an obstacle where the signal is partly blocked
A region where signals are always perfectly strong
The shadow cast by the receiver's antenna
items.explanation: A radio shadow is the blocked-off region behind a hill or building, much like a light shadow. It is usually weak rather than fully dark because diffraction leaks some signal in.

items.prompt: Which path between a transmitter and receiver generally gives the strongest signal?
choices: A path blocked by several buildings
A clear line-of-sight path with nothing in the way
A path that has to bend over two hills
A path passing through a tunnel
items.explanation: A clear line-of-sight path adds no blocking loss, so only the unavoidable spreading loss applies. Every obstacle that must be diffracted around or pushed through weakens the signal further.

items.prompt: How does the depth of the shadow affect the diffracted (bent-around) signal?
choices: The deeper into the shadow, the stronger the signal gets
The deeper into the shadow behind a sharp obstacle, the weaker the bent-around signal becomes
Depth in the shadow has no effect at all
The signal disappears instantly at the shadow's edge
items.explanation: Diffraction leaks some energy past the edge, but the farther you go into the shadow and the sharper the obstacle, the less of that bent-around signal survives. It fades gradually rather than vanishing at the edge.

items.prompt: Besides the steady weakening from spreading, what extra effect does the environment add to a signal's journey?
choices: It removes all weakening so the signal stays constant
Extra loss from blocking by hills, buildings, and trees, partly softened by diffraction into shadows
It speeds the signal up to make up for distance
It changes the signal into sound
items.explanation: The environment piles extra loss on top of spreading loss, mainly by blocking the path. Diffraction recovers a little by bending energy into the shadows, but the net effect of clutter is a faster-fading signal.

lessons.title: Multipath: Echoes, Fading, and Dead Spots
lessons.content:
Here is one of the strangest facts about radio: you can be standing still with a weak, crackly signal, take a single step sideways, and suddenly it is clear, or the other way around. Why would moving a few inches matter so much? The answer is **multipath**.

Radio waves bounce. They reflect off the ground, walls, cars, hills, and buildings, much like light reflecting off a mirror. So the signal usually does not reach the receiver by just one route. It arrives by **several paths at once**: the direct path, plus a copy that bounced off the ground, another off a nearby building, and so on. Each bounced copy travels a slightly different distance, so they arrive at slightly different times, like an **echo** arriving a moment after the original sound.

Now the twist. When these copies meet at the receiver, they can line up and **add together**, making the signal stronger, or they can be out of step and **cancel each other out**, making it weaker, even down to almost nothing. This adding and canceling is called **fading**, and a spot where the copies cancel is a **dead spot**.

Here is why a tiny movement matters: whether the copies add or cancel depends on the exact lengths of their paths. Move the receiver even a little and you change those path lengths, which can flip a dead spot into a strong spot. That is exactly why a step sideways can rescue a dropped call, and why holding a radio still in one place can leave it stuck in a fade.

Multipath is strongest where there are lots of reflecting surfaces, like inside buildings and dense cities, and it is a big reason indoor and downtown signals are so patchy. Engineers fight back by using more than one antenna or smarter signals, but the underlying cause is always the same: many bounced copies arriving and either helping or fighting each other.

**Recap:** Multipath means several reflected copies of a signal arrive at the receiver by different paths and slightly different times. Depending on how they line up, they add (stronger) or cancel (a dead spot), which is called fading. Because it depends on exact path lengths, moving even a little can change the signal completely.

items.prompt: What is multipath?
choices: A single signal traveling in a perfectly straight line
Several copies of the signal reaching the receiver by different routes after bouncing off surfaces
A path that only opens up at night
The route a technician walks to fix a tower
items.explanation: Multipath is the arrival of several copies of the same signal by different paths, created when waves reflect off the ground, walls, and buildings. It is the opposite of a single clean straight path.

items.prompt: Why can taking a single step sideways suddenly clear up a crackly signal?
choices: Walking generates extra power for the phone
Moving changes the exact path lengths of the bounced copies, which can flip a canceling dead spot into an adding strong spot
Your body blocks the bad signal when you move
The transmitter notices you moved and boosts itself
items.explanation: Whether the multipath copies add or cancel depends on their exact path lengths. A small move changes those lengths, so a dead spot (copies canceling) can become a strong spot (copies adding). This is fading, and it is why position matters so much.

items.prompt: When two arriving copies of a signal are out of step, what can happen?
choices: They always double the signal strength
They can cancel each other out, creating a weak spot or dead spot
They turn into two separate radio stations
They speed each other up
items.explanation: Out-of-step copies can cancel, weakening or nearly erasing the signal at that spot, a dead spot. In-step copies add and strengthen it. This adding and canceling is the heart of fading.

items.prompt: In radio, 'fading' refers to:
choices: A signal slowly losing color over time
The signal getting stronger or weaker as multipath copies add or cancel
The transmitter being switched off at sunset
Paint fading on an old antenna
items.explanation: Fading is the rise and fall of signal strength caused by multipath copies lining up (adding) or fighting (canceling). It is a direct result of several reflected paths arriving together.

items.prompt: Why do the bounced copies of a signal arrive at slightly different times?
choices: Some copies are sent later by the transmitter
Each bounced path travels a different distance, so it takes a slightly different amount of time, like an echo
The receiver speeds some copies up and slows others down
Time runs differently for reflected waves
items.explanation: A copy that bounces off a far wall travels farther than the direct copy, so it arrives a little later, just like a sound echo returning after the original. These small time differences are what let copies add or cancel.

items.prompt: A 'dead spot' for reception is best described as a place where:
choices: The transmitter has run out of power
Multipath copies arrive out of step and cancel, leaving almost no usable signal
All radio waves are permanently destroyed
The receiver's battery dies
items.explanation: A dead spot is a specific location where the bounced copies cancel each other. Step out of it and the copies may add instead. It is about how the copies line up at that exact place, not about transmitter power or batteries.

items.prompt: Where is multipath fading usually strongest?
choices: In wide-open empty deserts with no surfaces to reflect off
Inside buildings and dense cities, where many surfaces reflect the signal
High above the clouds where nothing exists
Only at the exact transmitter location
items.explanation: More reflecting surfaces mean more bounced copies, so cities and indoor spaces have the worst multipath. Open areas with few reflectors have far less, which is why rural line-of-sight links are steadier.

items.prompt: Which everyday experience is the best analogy for multipath copies arriving at different times?
choices: An echo in a canyon, where your shout comes back a moment after you make it
A balloon popping
Ice melting in a warm room
A compass needle pointing north
items.explanation: An echo is a reflected copy of your sound that took a longer path and so arrives later. Radio multipath copies do the same thing with reflected radio waves, and their timing decides whether they add or cancel.

items.prompt: One common way engineers fight multipath fading is to:
choices: Use more than one antenna, since a dead spot at one antenna is often a strong spot at another nearby
Turn the transmitter off and on repeatedly
Make the signal travel slower
Paint the antennas a darker color
items.explanation: Because fading depends on exact position, two antennas a short distance apart rarely sit in a dead spot at the same time. The receiver can pick whichever is stronger. This is one practical trick against multipath, no color or speed change needed.

lessons.title: High and Low Frequencies Travel Differently
lessons.content:
Not all radio signals travel the same way. A signal's **frequency** (how fast the wave wiggles, measured in hertz) strongly shapes its journey. As a broad rule with everyday consequences: **lower frequencies travel farther, bend around obstacles, and slip through walls more easily, while higher frequencies act more like a beam of light that is easily blocked.**

Think of sound for intuition. From a distant concert you hear the deep bass thumping through walls long before you make out the high notes. Low-pitched (low-frequency) sound bends around corners and passes through barriers; high-pitched sound is more easily blocked and absorbed. Radio behaves in a similar spirit: lower frequencies hug the ground and bend over hills and the curve of the horizon, reaching well beyond a straight line of sight. This is part of why AM radio (a lower frequency) can be picked up far away, and why submarines and old long-distance systems used very low frequencies.

Higher frequencies are the opposite. They travel in nearly straight lines, get blocked by buildings and hills, and do not penetrate walls well. They are more **line of sight**, meaning they really want a clear straight path. The trade-off is that higher frequencies can carry much more information, which is exactly why modern systems crave them.

This explains a hot topic: **5G mmWave**. Millimeter-wave (mmWave) signals are very high frequency. They can move enormous amounts of data, but they are blocked by walls, foliage, even rain and your own hand, and they fade fast with distance. Because each tower covers only a small area, mmWave networks need **many closely spaced towers** to give good coverage, while lower-frequency networks cover wide areas from far fewer towers.

**Recap:** Lower frequencies travel farther, bend around and over obstacles, and penetrate walls better; higher frequencies go in straight line-of-sight paths, are blocked more easily, but can carry more data. That is why very high frequencies like 5G mmWave need many closely spaced towers to cover an area.

items.prompt: What does a signal's 'frequency' describe?
choices: How loud the signal is
How fast the wave wiggles back and forth
How far the transmitter is from the receiver
What color the antenna is
items.explanation: Frequency is the rate of wiggling, measured in hertz. It is not loudness (that is more like power) or distance. Frequency strongly shapes how a signal travels through the world.

items.prompt: As a broad rule, how do low frequencies behave compared with high frequencies?
choices: Low frequencies travel farther and bend/penetrate better; high frequencies are more line-of-sight and easily blocked
Low frequencies are always blocked by everything; high frequencies pass through anything
Both behave identically in every situation
High frequencies bend around hills better than low ones
items.explanation: Lower frequencies bend around obstacles, follow the ground and horizon, and slip through walls more readily, so they reach farther. Higher frequencies act more like a light beam: straight, easily blocked, poor at penetrating. This is the key trade-off.

items.prompt: Why do lower frequencies generally travel and penetrate better than higher ones?
choices: They carry more power by nature
Their longer, slower wiggle bends around obstacles and through walls more easily, instead of being blocked like a beam
They are not affected by distance at all
They move faster than high frequencies
items.explanation: Lower-frequency waves tend to bend around and over obstacles and pass through barriers, so they reach beyond line of sight. Higher-frequency waves behave more like a straight beam that obstacles easily block. All radio waves travel at the same speed; the difference is in how they interact with obstacles.

items.prompt: From a distant concert you hear the deep bass through walls before the high notes. Why is this a useful analogy for radio?
choices: It is unrelated; sound and radio share nothing
Low-pitched (low-frequency) sound bends and penetrates better than high-pitched sound, mirroring how low radio frequencies travel and penetrate better
It shows high frequencies are always louder
It proves walls amplify music
items.explanation: The bass (low frequency) gets through walls and around corners while the treble (high frequency) is blocked. Radio frequencies follow the same spirit: lower ones penetrate and bend better, higher ones are more easily blocked.

items.prompt: Why does 5G mmWave (very high frequency) need many closely spaced towers?
choices: Because mmWave signals travel for hundreds of miles from one tower
Because mmWave is easily blocked by walls, foliage, and even rain and fades fast, so each tower covers only a small area
Because high frequencies refuse to carry data
Because towers are cheaper when crowded together
items.explanation: mmWave's very high frequency means it is blocked by almost anything and weakens quickly, so each tower's reach is short. Covering an area therefore takes many closely spaced towers, unlike low-frequency networks that cover wide areas from few towers.

items.prompt: What is the main advantage that pushes engineers toward higher frequencies despite their poor reach?
choices: They can carry much more information (data)
They never weaken with distance
They pass through walls more easily
They bend around hills better
items.explanation: Higher frequencies can carry far more data, which is why modern high-speed systems want them. The cost is that they are blocked easily and do not reach as far, so they trade coverage for capacity.

items.prompt: Why can AM radio (a lower frequency) often be received from very far away?
choices: Because low frequencies hug the ground and bend over the horizon, reaching beyond a straight line of sight
Because AM transmitters are always the most powerful on Earth
Because low frequencies travel faster than light
Because AM signals never weaken
items.explanation: Lower frequencies follow the ground and bend over the curve of the horizon, so they reach past where a straight line of sight ends. That is a big part of AM radio's long range, separate from raw transmitter power.

items.prompt: A high-frequency link runs between two buildings, and a new building goes up in between. What is the likely result?
choices: No effect, since high frequencies pass through buildings easily
The link is badly degraded or lost, because high frequencies are line-of-sight and easily blocked
The signal gets stronger from the extra reflection
The frequency automatically lowers itself
items.explanation: High frequencies need a clear straight path. A new building blocking the line of sight can break the link, because these signals do not bend around or punch through obstacles well. Lower frequencies would cope better.

items.prompt: Why might a phone on a lower-frequency band keep a signal deep inside a building when a high-frequency band drops out?
choices: Lower frequencies penetrate walls and floors better than higher frequencies
Lower frequencies are immune to all obstacles
Higher frequencies are turned off indoors by law
The building blocks only low frequencies
items.explanation: Lower frequencies slip through walls and floors more readily, so they reach deeper indoors. Higher frequencies are blocked by the same structure. This is why networks often rely on lower bands for indoor coverage.

lessons.title: Sky, Weather, and the Horizon
lessons.content:
The sky itself and the weather shape how far a signal travels. Three big effects round out the propagation story: the bouncing sky, rain, and the curve of the Earth.

High above us is the **ionosphere**, a layer of the upper atmosphere that the Sun charges up during the day. For certain lower frequencies, especially **shortwave**, the ionosphere acts like a mirror in the sky: a signal aimed upward is **bounced back down** far over the horizon. By skipping off the ionosphere, a shortwave signal can travel around the curve of the Earth and be heard thousands of miles away. This is **ionospheric skip**. It changes between **day and night** because the Sun changes the layer. At night the lower part of the ionosphere fades, letting signals bounce more cleanly off the higher part, which is why AM radio stations travel much farther after dark and you can pick up distant stations at night that vanish by day.

Weather matters too, mostly at high frequencies. Falling rain can **absorb and scatter** high-frequency signals, weakening them. This is called **rain fade**, and it is why a satellite TV picture can break up during a heavy downpour. The higher the frequency, the worse rain fade tends to be, which is another limit on high-frequency links.

The air can also bend signals in a special way called **ducting**: sometimes layers of warm and cool air act like an invisible pipe that traps a signal and carries it far beyond its normal range. Ducting is occasional and weather-driven, so it can briefly let distant signals appear where they usually cannot.

Finally, because the Earth is round, straight line-of-sight signals can only reach so far before the ground curves away below them, the **radio horizon**. Putting antennas up high on towers or hills lets them see farther over the curve before the horizon cuts them off, which is why broadcast towers are tall and on high ground.

**Recap:** The ionosphere can bounce shortwave signals around the Earth, and this skip changes between day and night (AM travels farther at night). Rain absorbs high-frequency signals (rain fade), ducting can occasionally trap signals and carry them far, and the round Earth sets a radio horizon that taller antennas can see past.

items.prompt: What is the ionosphere, and what can it do for radio?
choices: A layer of the upper atmosphere that can bounce certain signals back down to Earth, far over the horizon
A type of antenna mounted on satellites
The metal core at the center of the Earth
A kind of weather radar
items.explanation: The ionosphere is a Sun-charged layer high in the atmosphere. For lower frequencies like shortwave it acts like a sky mirror, bouncing signals back down far beyond the horizon. That bounce is ionospheric skip.

items.prompt: How can a shortwave signal be heard thousands of miles away, beyond the curve of the Earth?
choices: It tunnels straight through the planet
It bounces off the ionosphere back down to Earth, skipping over the horizon
It travels faster than light to get there
It is relayed by underground cables
items.explanation: Ionospheric skip lets the signal reflect off the high atmosphere and come back down far away, hopping over the Earth's curve. It does not pass through the planet or exceed light speed.

items.prompt: Why does AM radio often travel much farther at night than during the day?
choices: Transmitters legally double their power after sunset
At night the Sun stops charging the lower ionosphere, so signals bounce more cleanly off the higher layer and skip farther
Radio waves prefer the dark
There is less traffic noise at night
items.explanation: The Sun changes the ionosphere. After dark the absorbing lower part fades, letting signals reflect well off the higher part and travel far. This day/night change is why distant AM stations appear at night and fade by day.

items.prompt: What is 'rain fade'?
choices: Rain washing the paint off an antenna
Falling rain absorbing and scattering a high-frequency signal, weakening it
A signal that only works when it rains
Rain making low frequencies stronger
items.explanation: Rain fade is the weakening of (mostly high-frequency) signals when raindrops absorb and scatter them. It is why satellite TV can break up in a heavy downpour. It has nothing to do with paint.

items.prompt: Rain fade tends to be worse for which signals?
choices: Higher-frequency signals
Lower-frequency signals
It affects all frequencies exactly equally
Only signals sent at night
items.explanation: The higher the frequency, the more strongly raindrops absorb and scatter the signal, so high-frequency links suffer the most rain fade. Lower frequencies are far less bothered by rain.

items.prompt: What is 'ducting' in radio propagation?
choices: Running signals through metal air-conditioning ducts
Layers of warm and cool air acting like an invisible pipe that traps a signal and carries it far beyond its normal range
A permanent feature that always doubles a signal's range
A way to block all signals during storms
items.explanation: Ducting happens when air layers trap a signal and guide it much farther than usual, like an invisible pipe. It is occasional and weather-driven, so it briefly extends range rather than always doing so.

items.prompt: What is the 'radio horizon'?
choices: The farthest a straight line-of-sight signal can reach before the curve of the Earth blocks it
The line where the ionosphere meets space
The maximum frequency a radio can use
The edge of a city's coverage map
items.explanation: Because the Earth is round, a straight signal eventually meets the ground curving away and can go no farther in a straight line. That limit is the radio horizon. Sky bounce and ducting can reach beyond it, but plain line of sight cannot.

items.prompt: Why are broadcast and cell towers usually built tall and on high ground?
choices: To stay above the rain so they never face rain fade
So their antennas can see farther over the curve of the Earth before the radio horizon cuts them off
Because radio waves only work at high altitude
To get closer to the ionosphere for bouncing
items.explanation: Raising an antenna pushes back the radio horizon, letting line-of-sight signals reach farther before the Earth's curve blocks them. Height is about extending the horizon, not escaping rain or touching the ionosphere.

items.prompt: Which effect lets a signal reach far beyond the normal line-of-sight radio horizon?
choices: Spreading loss
Bouncing off the ionosphere (ionospheric skip)
Rain fade
A shorter antenna
items.explanation: Ionospheric skip bounces a signal off the high atmosphere back down far away, reaching well past the line-of-sight horizon. Spreading loss and rain fade only weaken signals, and a shorter antenna would see a nearer horizon, not a farther one.

items.prompt: Compared with a wide-open rural area, a dense city or indoor space tends to give:
choices: A steadier, stronger signal because buildings boost power
A patchier signal, because blocking and many reflections (multipath fading) make strength vary spot to spot
No signal at all, ever
Exactly the same reception everywhere
items.explanation: Cities and indoor spaces pile on blocking and lots of reflections, so signals are shadowed in places and fade in and out from multipath. Open rural areas with clear paths and few reflectors give steadier reception.
